title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who describe a major decision they had to make and how they approached it. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: A Fork in the Road: Four People on Making Important Life Decisions

texts.B:
### Diane, 44, HR Manager
The biggest decision of my life was leaving a well-paid corporate job to retrain as a family therapist in my early forties. [43]Most people around me thought I was being reckless, but I had spent years helping employees through personal crises at work and knew that counselling was where my real skills and passion lay.[/43] The financial hit was considerable and I took on part-time work to cover my studies, but I never once doubted the direction. [47]What I hadn't anticipated was the grief I would feel at leaving a career I had worked so hard to build, even though I knew the new path was right.[/47] Acknowledging that complexity has since helped me support clients going through their own transitions. [51]Three years on, working with families in genuinely difficult situations, I feel a level of purpose I simply never felt behind a corporate desk.[/51]
texts.D:
### Sam, 29, Software Developer
[44]At twenty-six, I turned down a promotion in London to move to Berlin, where I barely spoke the language and knew almost nobody.[/44] Everyone assumed I was running away from something, but I was running towards something – a slower pace of life and a city whose culture I had admired from afar for years. [48]The first year was genuinely isolating in a way I hadn't prepared for – building friendships from scratch as an adult is far harder than it sounds.[/48] I threw myself into language classes and local sports clubs to meet people, which eventually worked. [52]Now I can't imagine going back, and I sometimes wonder why it took me so long to be brave enough to make the move.[/52]
texts.A:
### Helena, 55, Journalist
I was a foreign correspondent for twenty years, travelling constantly and covering conflicts and disasters around the world. [45]The decision to stop came when I realised I was missing the small but important moments in my children's lives, and I decided no story was worth that cost.[/45] Transitioning to editing was humbling – moving from the field to a desk felt like a demotion even though it was technically a promotion. [49]What helped me most was speaking to other journalists who had made similar transitions and realising that the sense of loss I felt was widely shared, not a sign of weakness.[/49] I now run a mentoring programme for young foreign correspondents, which keeps me connected to reporting without the same personal cost.
texts.C:
### Kwame, 38, Chef
[46]Opening my own restaurant meant walking away from a head chef position at a prestigious hotel, which took three years of persuasion before my family accepted it wasn't sheer foolishness.[/46] I had saved for five years and rewritten my business plan countless times before finally signing the lease. The restaurant nearly failed in its first six months – I had underestimated how different running a business was from running a kitchen. [50]I hired a business mentor whose advice saved me from several costly mistakes I would never have anticipated on my own.[/50] The restaurant is now in its fourth year and recently won a local dining award, which still feels slightly unreal.

questions.question: went against the advice of those around them when deciding to change direction?
questions.explanation: Diane says most people thought she was being reckless, but she knew counselling was where her real passion lay.

questions.question: relocated to a place where they had few connections and limited language skills?
questions.explanation: Sam turned down a promotion to move to Berlin, where he barely spoke the language and knew almost nobody.

questions.question: made their decision primarily out of concern for their responsibilities to their family?
questions.explanation: Helena stopped being a foreign correspondent when she realised she was missing important moments in her children's lives.

questions.question: describes a long period of preparation before finally committing to a major step?
questions.explanation: Kwame spent three years persuading his family, five years saving, and rewrote his business plan many times before signing the lease.

questions.question: experienced an emotional reaction to their decision that they had not expected?
questions.explanation: Diane says she hadn't anticipated the grief she would feel at leaving her old career, even though she knew the new path was right.

questions.question: found the early period after their decision more socially challenging than they had anticipated?
questions.explanation: Sam says the first year in Berlin was genuinely isolating and that building friendships from scratch as an adult is far harder than it sounds.

questions.question: felt reassured upon learning that others had gone through a very similar experience?
questions.explanation: Helena says talking to other journalists who had made similar transitions helped her realise the sense of loss she felt was widely shared.

questions.question: benefited significantly from the guidance of an experienced person during a critical phase?
questions.explanation: Kwame hired a business mentor whose advice saved him from several costly mistakes he would not have foreseen alone.

questions.question: expresses a strong sense of meaning and fulfilment in the life they ultimately chose?
questions.explanation: Diane says she now feels a level of purpose working with families that she simply never felt in her corporate career.

questions.question: wonders in retrospect why they were not bolder sooner?
questions.explanation: Sam says he can't imagine going back to London and sometimes wonders why it took him so long to be brave enough to make the move.
